Authors: Lu Qiu; Yi Chen; Yuying Ge; Yixiao Ge; Ying Shan; Xihui Liu

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02826-y

Relevance: 0.772 (consider)

Abstract Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has recently demonstrated superior multimodal comprehension abilities, heralding a new era for artificial general intelligence (AGI). However, achieving AGI necessitates more than just comprehension. A crucial capability required is effective planning in diverse scenarios, which involves making reasonable decisions based on complex environments to solve real-world problems. Despite its importance, the planning abilities of current MLLMs in varied scenarios remain underexplored, leaving a significant gap in our understanding of their full potential. In this paper, we introduce EgoPlan-Bench2, a rigorous and comprehensive benchmark designed to assess the planning capabilities of MLLMs across a wide range of real-world scenarios . EgoPlan-Bench2 encompasses everyday tasks spanning 4 major domains and 24 detailed scenarios, closely aligned with human daily life. It is constructed through a semi-automatic process utilizing egocentric videos, complemented by manual verification. Grounded in a first-person perspective, it mirrors the way humans approach problem-solving in everyday life. We evaluate 25 competitive MLLMs and provide an in-depth analysis of their limitations, revealing that they face significant challenges in real-world planning. To diagnose the underlying bottlenecks, we investigate the effectiveness of various prompts via a training-free multimodal prompting method. We find that MLLMs’ planning performance on EgoPlan-Bench2 is critically dependent on temporally structured action sequences in historical task progress and interactions between objects and humans in current observation state. This dependency also underscores the necessity for strong reasoning abilities to integrate diverse multimodal cues and analysis before making final decision. Building on this insight, we demonstrate that EgoPlan-Bench2 is also an effective video reasoning benchmark. Experiments with Gemini-2.5-Flash and a post-trained Qwen-2.5-VL confirm its ability to distinguish between models with and without explicit deliberate reasoning mechanisms, showcasing the tangible impact of DeepSeek-R1 paradigm reasoning on planning tasks. We have made data and code available at https://qiulu66.github.io/egoplanbench2/ .

item.title: Exploring MLLMs Perception of Network Visualization Principles
item.dc:creator: Jacob Miller
Markus Wallinger
Ludwig Felder
Timo Brand
Henry Förster
Johannes Zink
Chunyang Chen
Stephen Kobourov
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
item.description: In this paper, we test whether Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can match human-subject performance in tasks involving the perception of properties in network layouts. Specifically, we replicate a human-subject experiment about perceiving quality (namely stress) in network layouts using GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5 and Qwen2.5. Our experiments show that giving MLLMs the same study information as trained human participants yields performance comparable to that of human experts and exceeds that of untrained non-experts. Additionally, we show that prompt engineering that deviates from the human-subject experiment can lead to better-than-human performance in some settings. Interestingly, like human subjects, the MLLMs seem to rely on visual proxies rather than computing the actual value of stress, indicating some sense or facsimile of perception. Explanations from the models are similar to those used by the human participants (e.g., an even distribution of nodes and uniform edge lengths). Published: 2026-04-08T20:05:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics Score: 0.772 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jacob Miller; Markus Wallinger; Ludwig Felder; Timo Brand; Henry Förster; Johannes Zink; Chunyang Chen; Stephen Kobourov

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics

DOI: 10.1109/tvcg.2026.3681983

Relevance: 0.772 (consider)

In this paper, we test whether Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can match human-subject performance in tasks involving the perception of properties in network layouts. Specifically, we replicate a human-subject experiment about perceiving quality (namely stress) in network layouts using GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5 and Qwen2.5. Our experiments show that giving MLLMs the same study information as trained human participants yields performance comparable to that of human experts and exceeds that of untrained non-experts. Additionally, we show that prompt engineering that deviates from the human-subject experiment can lead to better-than-human performance in some settings. Interestingly, like human subjects, the MLLMs seem to rely on visual proxies rather than computing the actual value of stress, indicating some sense or facsimile of perception. Explanations from the models are similar to those used by the human participants (e.g., an even distribution of nodes and uniform edge lengths).

item.title: PV-LiverNet: A Multi-phase Temporal Fusion Framework Unifying Voxel Semantics and Point Geometry for Liver Tumor Segmentation and Classification
item.dc:creator: Rong Li
Zimu Wang
Gang Liu
Deyu Fan
Fule Deng
Shuyao He
Yangjie Wang
Meng Xu
item.prism:publicationName: Information Fusion
item.description: Precise segmentation and differential diagnosis of liver tumors are critical for treatment planning. However, existing methods often treat spatial and temporal features in isolation, hindering effective information fusion between the semantic richness of volumetric data and the geometric precision of surface boundaries. To bridge this gap, we propose PV-LiverNet, a novel framework that achieves unified analysis through deep multi-modal information fusion. The architecture is driven by two synergistic streams: a Hemodynamic-Aware Voxel Stream with Temporal Channel Attention (TCA) to capture phase-dependent patterns and a Topology-Preserving Point Stream utilizing Gradient-Guided Adaptive Sampling (GGAS) to extract high-frequency boundary details. Central to our approach is the Bi-directional Spatio-Temporal Fusion (BSTF) module, which serves as a core engine for information fusion, dynamically integrating these representations—injecting semantic context into sparse points while leveraging geometric cues to sharpen voxel features. Extensive experiments on a large-scale multi-phase Computed Tomography (CT) dataset and the LiTS benchmark demonstrate that this fusion-centric design significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. It achieves superior boundary adherence (46% reduction in 95% Hausdorff Distance) and diagnostic accuracy, providing a robust solution for unified liver tumor analysis. Published: 2026-04-08T16:41:57+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.771 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rong Li; Zimu Wang; Gang Liu; Deyu Fan; Fule Deng; Shuyao He; Yangjie Wang; Meng Xu

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104357

Relevance: 0.771 (consider)

Precise segmentation and differential diagnosis of liver tumors are critical for treatment planning. However, existing methods often treat spatial and temporal features in isolation, hindering effective information fusion between the semantic richness of volumetric data and the geometric precision of surface boundaries. To bridge this gap, we propose PV-LiverNet, a novel framework that achieves unified analysis through deep multi-modal information fusion. The architecture is driven by two synergistic streams: a Hemodynamic-Aware Voxel Stream with Temporal Channel Attention (TCA) to capture phase-dependent patterns and a Topology-Preserving Point Stream utilizing Gradient-Guided Adaptive Sampling (GGAS) to extract high-frequency boundary details. Central to our approach is the Bi-directional Spatio-Temporal Fusion (BSTF) module, which serves as a core engine for information fusion, dynamically integrating these representations—injecting semantic context into sparse points while leveraging geometric cues to sharpen voxel features. Extensive experiments on a large-scale multi-phase Computed Tomography (CT) dataset and the LiTS benchmark demonstrate that this fusion-centric design significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. It achieves superior boundary adherence (46% reduction in 95% Hausdorff Distance) and diagnostic accuracy, providing a robust solution for unified liver tumor analysis.

item.title: Acoustic-optical Joint Underwater Object Detection with Multi-Modality Correlation Features Matching Network
item.dc:creator: Meiyan Zhang
Yuxin Lin
Jifeng Zhu
Mai Wang
Wenyu Cai
item.prism:publicationName: Neural Networks
item.description: Underwater object detection is of significant importance for exploring underwater world, however, there is little research focusing on multi-modality detection in this field. Due to weak image alignment of underwater camera and sonar device, there is big challenge for object detection with underwater acoustic-optical images. On the basis of images from underwater camera and sonar device, this paper proposes a novel acoustic-optical joint multi-modality detection model (AOMNet in short) for underwater object detection. At the feature level of AOMNet model, we propose CPMFM based on cross-feature and predicted position, which utilizes contour and texture similarity between different modalities, as well as the predicted position parameters, to optimize the generation strategy of candidate regions and guide the model to generate more accurate bounding frames. At the decision-making level of AOMNet model, we use PLFM+BRM strategy to register multi-modality, and implement region matching through morphological calculation and affine transformation of mask to ensure more accurate boundary box regression. Finally, our method is validated on self-collected dataset of sonar and underwater camera. The proposed AOMNet algorithm shows significant advantages over existing single and multi-modality detection models, with mAP of 81.3%. Published: 2026-04-08T23:16:18+00:00 Venue: Neural Networks Score: 0.769 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Meiyan Zhang; Yuxin Lin; Jifeng Zhu; Mai Wang; Wenyu Cai

Journal: Neural Networks

DOI: 10.1016/j.neunet.2026.108975

Relevance: 0.769 (consider)

Underwater object detection is of significant importance for exploring underwater world, however, there is little research focusing on multi-modality detection in this field. Due to weak image alignment of underwater camera and sonar device, there is big challenge for object detection with underwater acoustic-optical images. On the basis of images from underwater camera and sonar device, this paper proposes a novel acoustic-optical joint multi-modality detection model (AOMNet in short) for underwater object detection. At the feature level of AOMNet model, we propose CPMFM based on cross-feature and predicted position, which utilizes contour and texture similarity between different modalities, as well as the predicted position parameters, to optimize the generation strategy of candidate regions and guide the model to generate more accurate bounding frames. At the decision-making level of AOMNet model, we use PLFM+BRM strategy to register multi-modality, and implement region matching through morphological calculation and affine transformation of mask to ensure more accurate boundary box regression. Finally, our method is validated on self-collected dataset of sonar and underwater camera. The proposed AOMNet algorithm shows significant advantages over existing single and multi-modality detection models, with mAP of 81.3%.

item.title: Spatial Meta-Learning-Based Representation for Unseen Geographic Entities
item.dc:creator: Shengwen Li
Zhouzheng Xu
Renyao Chen
Jiarui Zhu
Yaqin Ye
Shunping Zhou
Hong Yao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: Geographic entity representation learning (GERL) is an emerging method that represents natural, administrative divisions, road networks, and points of interest (POIs) in a low-dimensional continuous vector space. GERL provides a fundamental approach for geographic entities to underpin a variety of intelligent applications by learning their representation vectors to capture the semantics and interactions of the entities. Previous GERL methods mainly focus on the representation learning of the geographic entities that are seen at the time of training, which struggle to accurately generate representation vectors for the growing number of unseen geographic entities that were not involved in model training. To address this issue, this article proposes spatial meta-learning-based representation learning (SMRL), which integrates spatial subgraphs and meta-learning to improve the representation vectors of unseen geographic entities. Specifically, SMRL first designs a spatial-aware subgraph sampling module based on attributes and relationships of geographic entities to divide entities into spatial subgraphs. It develops a local-level representation module to learn entity features at the subgraph level. Finally, SMRL proposes a meta-learning-driven representation strategy that integrates meta-learning to learn the representation of unseen geographic entities. Extensive experiments show that the proposed SMRL method outperforms baselines with both higher accuracy and higher computational efficiency. This study provides new explorations for the representation of unseen geographic entities and offers methodological References for the various geographic applications. Published: 2026-04-08T20:06:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.769 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shengwen Li; Zhouzheng Xu; Renyao Chen; Jiarui Zhu; Yaqin Ye; Shunping Zhou; Hong Yao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tnnls.2026.3679789

Relevance: 0.769 (consider)

Geographic entity representation learning (GERL) is an emerging method that represents natural, administrative divisions, road networks, and points of interest (POIs) in a low-dimensional continuous vector space. GERL provides a fundamental approach for geographic entities to underpin a variety of intelligent applications by learning their representation vectors to capture the semantics and interactions of the entities. Previous GERL methods mainly focus on the representation learning of the geographic entities that are seen at the time of training, which struggle to accurately generate representation vectors for the growing number of unseen geographic entities that were not involved in model training. To address this issue, this article proposes spatial meta-learning-based representation learning (SMRL), which integrates spatial subgraphs and meta-learning to improve the representation vectors of unseen geographic entities. Specifically, SMRL first designs a spatial-aware subgraph sampling module based on attributes and relationships of geographic entities to divide entities into spatial subgraphs. It develops a local-level representation module to learn entity features at the subgraph level. Finally, SMRL proposes a meta-learning-driven representation strategy that integrates meta-learning to learn the representation of unseen geographic entities. Extensive experiments show that the proposed SMRL method outperforms baselines with both higher accuracy and higher computational efficiency. This study provides new explorations for the representation of unseen geographic entities and offers methodological References for the various geographic applications.

item.title: Cross-view localization via redundant sliced observations and a-contrario validation
item.dc:creator: Yongjun Zhang
Mingtao Xiong
Yi Wan
Gui-Song Xia
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Cross-view localization (CVL) matches ground-level images with aerial references to determine the geo-position of a camera, enabling smart vehicles to self-localize offline in GNSS-denied environments. However, most CVL methods output only a single observation, the camera pose, and lack the redundant observations required by surveying principles, making it challenging to assess localization reliability through the mutual validation of observational data. To tackle this, we introduce Slice-Loc, a two-stage method featuring an a-contrario reliability validation for CVL. Instead of using the query image as a single input, Slice-Loc divides it into sub-images and estimates the 3-DoF pose for each slice, creating redundant and independent observations. Then, a geometric rigidity formula is proposed to filter out the erroneous 3-DoF poses, and the inliers are merged to generate the final camera pose. Furthermore, we propose a model that quantifies the meaningfulness of localization by estimating the number of false alarms (NFA), according to the distribution of the locations of the sliced images. By eliminating gross errors, Slice-Loc boosts localization accuracy and effectively detects failures. After filtering out mislocalizations, Slice-Loc reduces the proportion of errors exceeding 10 m to under 3%. In cross-city tests on the DReSS-D dataset, Slice-Loc cuts the mean localization error from 4.47 m to 1.86 m and the mean orientation error from 3 . 42 ° " role="presentation"> 3 . 42 ° 3 . 42 ° to 1 . 24 ° " role="presentation"> 1 . 24 ° 1 . 24 ° , outperforming state-of-the-art methods. Code and dataset will be available at: https://github.com/bnothing/Slice-Loc . Published: 2026-04-09T16:55:08+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.766 (consider)

Authors: Yongjun Zhang; Mingtao Xiong; Yi Wan; Gui-Song Xia

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.014

Relevance: 0.766 (consider)

Cross-view localization (CVL) matches ground-level images with aerial references to determine the geo-position of a camera, enabling smart vehicles to self-localize offline in GNSS-denied environments. However, most CVL methods output only a single observation, the camera pose, and lack the redundant observations required by surveying principles, making it challenging to assess localization reliability through the mutual validation of observational data. To tackle this, we introduce Slice-Loc, a two-stage method featuring an a-contrario reliability validation for CVL. Instead of using the query image as a single input, Slice-Loc divides it into sub-images and estimates the 3-DoF pose for each slice, creating redundant and independent observations. Then, a geometric rigidity formula is proposed to filter out the erroneous 3-DoF poses, and the inliers are merged to generate the final camera pose. Furthermore, we propose a model that quantifies the meaningfulness of localization by estimating the number of false alarms (NFA), according to the distribution of the locations of the sliced images. By eliminating gross errors, Slice-Loc boosts localization accuracy and effectively detects failures. After filtering out mislocalizations, Slice-Loc reduces the proportion of errors exceeding 10 m to under 3%. In cross-city tests on the DReSS-D dataset, Slice-Loc cuts the mean localization error from 4.47 m to 1.86 m and the mean orientation error from 3 . 42 ° " role="presentation"> 3 . 42 ° 3 . 42 ° to 1 . 24 ° " role="presentation"> 1 . 24 ° 1 . 24 ° , outperforming state-of-the-art methods. Code and dataset will be available at: https://github.com/bnothing/Slice-Loc .

item.title: KAN-UG: Kolmogorov-Arnold Network-Based Difference Feature Enhancement Method with Uncertainty Guidance for Change Detection
item.dc:creator: Shaoxiong Lin
Tao Lei
Hailong Ning
Xingwu Wang
Xuan Wang
Daqi Liu
Maoguo Gong
Asoke K. Nandi
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Although existing semi-supervised methods for remote sensing change detection (RSCD) have shown promising performance with limited labeled data, they still face two key challenges. First, due to the complexity of remote sensing (RS) scenarios, these methods often struggle to capture subtle changes of ground objects and exhibit limited capacity to model the nonlinear characteristics of complex objects, thereby restricting detection accuracy. Second, existing methods fail to adequately exploit the collaboration between labeled data and unlabeled data, which tends to cause error accumulation and consequently leads to insufficient representation of challenging regions. Recently, Kolmogorov-Arnold Network has attracted increasing attention for its strong capability in modeling complex nonlinear data distributions. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-based difference feature enhancement method with uncertainty guidance (KAN-UG) for semi-supervised RSCD. First, a KAN-based difference feature enhancement module (K-DFEM) is constructed to better capture subtle changes in complex scenarios. This module enhances the discriminability of features in complex scenarios through learnable nonlinear activation and progressive multi-scale fusion. Second, an uncertainty-guided adaptive learning (UAL) mechanism is designed to prevent the model from being misled during optimization. This mechanism promotes effective collaboration between supervised and unsupervised learning by improving the utilization of challenging regions in labeled data and mitigating error propagation from challenging regions in unlabeled data. Experimental results on four widely used datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art semi-supervised change detection (SSCD) methods across diverse and challenging scenarios. In particular, with only 5% labeled ratio, our method achieves IoU improvements of 7.42% and 3.70% over the best competing method on the CDD a... Authors: Shaoxiong Lin; Tao Lei; Hailong Ning; Xingwu Wang; Xuan Wang; Daqi Liu; Maoguo Gong; Asoke K. Nandi

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3681936

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

Although existing semi-supervised methods for remote sensing change detection (RSCD) have shown promising performance with limited labeled data, they still face two key challenges. First, due to the complexity of remote sensing (RS) scenarios, these methods often struggle to capture subtle changes of ground objects and exhibit limited capacity to model the nonlinear characteristics of complex objects, thereby restricting detection accuracy. Second, existing methods fail to adequately exploit the collaboration between labeled data and unlabeled data, which tends to cause error accumulation and consequently leads to insufficient representation of challenging regions. Recently, Kolmogorov-Arnold Network has attracted increasing attention for its strong capability in modeling complex nonlinear data distributions. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-based difference feature enhancement method with uncertainty guidance (KAN-UG) for semi-supervised RSCD. First, a KAN-based difference feature enhancement module (K-DFEM) is constructed to better capture subtle changes in complex scenarios. This module enhances the discriminability of features in complex scenarios through learnable nonlinear activation and progressive multi-scale fusion. Second, an uncertainty-guided adaptive learning (UAL) mechanism is designed to prevent the model from being misled during optimization. This mechanism promotes effective collaboration between supervised and unsupervised learning by improving the utilization of challenging regions in labeled data and mitigating error propagation from challenging regions in unlabeled data. Experimental results on four widely used datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art semi-supervised change detection (SSCD) methods across diverse and challenging scenarios. In particular, with only 5% labeled ratio, our method achieves IoU improvements of 7.42% and 3.70% over the best competing method on the CDD a...

item.title: Hyperspectral computational imaging: A comprehensive review
item.dc:creator: Wei He
He Huang
Yu Sheng
Zhehui Wu
Guang Jin
Jianya Gong
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Conventional hyperspectral imaging (HSI) is fundamentally constrained by the trade-off between temporal resolution and scanning mechanisms, rendering it unsuitable for dynamic scene capture. Hyperspectral Computational Imaging (HCI) has emerged as a transformative solution, integrating front-end optical encoding with back-end computational decoding to reconstruct high-dimensional data cubes from compressed multiplexed measurements. This paper presents a systematic and comprehensive review of the HCI landscape. We first categorize mainstream optical architectures, including Coded Aperture Snapshot Spectral Imagers (CASSI), Hyperspectral Single-Pixel Imaging (HS-SPI), Spectral Filter Array-based systems and Diffractive Coded Imaging (DCI). A significant portion of this review is dedicated to the evolution of reconstruction algorithms, tracing the trajectory from traditional model-based optimization relying on hand-crafted priors (e.g., sparsity, Total Variation, low-rankness) to modern data-driven deep learning paradigms. We provide an in-depth analysis of state-of-the-art (SOTA) methodologies, specifically End-to-End (E2E) networks, Plug-and-Play (PnP) frameworks, and Deep Unfolding Networks (DUNs). Furthermore, we discuss the integration of emerging generative models, including Transformers, Diffusion Models, and Mamba, which are redefining the boundaries of reconstruction fidelity. Finally, we outline open challenges and future trends shaping the next generation of intelligent HCI systems. We look ahead to cognitive active imaging, charting a path through optical neural computing, uncertainty quantification, and universal spectral foundation models to facilitate HCI deployment in dynamic scientific and industrial applications. Published: 2026-04-08T11:16:45+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.765 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wei He; He Huang; Yu Sheng; Zhehui Wu; Guang Jin; Jianya Gong

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.041

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

Conventional hyperspectral imaging (HSI) is fundamentally constrained by the trade-off between temporal resolution and scanning mechanisms, rendering it unsuitable for dynamic scene capture. Hyperspectral Computational Imaging (HCI) has emerged as a transformative solution, integrating front-end optical encoding with back-end computational decoding to reconstruct high-dimensional data cubes from compressed multiplexed measurements. This paper presents a systematic and comprehensive review of the HCI landscape. We first categorize mainstream optical architectures, including Coded Aperture Snapshot Spectral Imagers (CASSI), Hyperspectral Single-Pixel Imaging (HS-SPI), Spectral Filter Array-based systems and Diffractive Coded Imaging (DCI). A significant portion of this review is dedicated to the evolution of reconstruction algorithms, tracing the trajectory from traditional model-based optimization relying on hand-crafted priors (e.g., sparsity, Total Variation, low-rankness) to modern data-driven deep learning paradigms. We provide an in-depth analysis of state-of-the-art (SOTA) methodologies, specifically End-to-End (E2E) networks, Plug-and-Play (PnP) frameworks, and Deep Unfolding Networks (DUNs). Furthermore, we discuss the integration of emerging generative models, including Transformers, Diffusion Models, and Mamba, which are redefining the boundaries of reconstruction fidelity. Finally, we outline open challenges and future trends shaping the next generation of intelligent HCI systems. We look ahead to cognitive active imaging, charting a path through optical neural computing, uncertainty quantification, and universal spectral foundation models to facilitate HCI deployment in dynamic scientific and industrial applications.

item.title: 具身智能前沿展望：数据、模型与系统演进
item.dc:creator: Mu Yao
Zhao Hao
Hu Ruizhen
Zhang Li
Li Hongyang
Yang Jiaolong
Wang Jingbo
Han Lei
Su Yongfeng
Xu Kai
Yang Yi
Li Jiang
Dai Ruoli
Chen Baoquan
Liu Yebin
Yi Li
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Image and Graphics
item.description: 具身智能作为人工智能发展的关键领域，正面临数据异构性、强物理约束及交互昂贵等挑战，难以直接复制大语言模型的“大规模预训练+规模定律”范式。本文从数据、模型、系统与评测四个维度全面梳理了具身智能的前沿技术演进。在数据层面，文章提出了“数据金字塔”结构，主张利用底层庞大的仿真与互联网视频数据构建物理常识，通过中层人类交互数据进行行为映射，最终以顶层少量真机数据实现技能落地。在模型层面，探讨了主流视觉-语言-动作模型（VLA）的扩展瓶颈，并指出“世界模型”作为具身预训练的新方向，能够通过模拟环境动力学与未来预演，赋予智能体更强的物理直觉与泛化能力。在系统层面，观察到架构正从单一端到端模型向类操作系统的“分层架构”演进，实现高层语义规划与底层运动控制的解耦。最后，本文审视了当前评测体系在真实性与可复现性上的挑战，并对行走与操作一体化及具身智能“ImageNet时刻”的到来进行了展望。 Published: 2026-04-08T02:24:36+00:00 Venue: Journal of Image and Graphics Score: 0.764 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mu Yao; Zhao Hao; Hu Ruizhen; Zhang Li; Li Hongyang; Yang Jiaolong; Wang Jingbo; Han Lei; Su Yongfeng; Xu Kai; Yang Yi; Li Jiang; Dai Ruoli; Chen Baoquan; Liu Yebin; Yi Li

Journal: Journal of Image and Graphics

DOI: 10.11834/jig.260059

Relevance: 0.764 (consider)

具身智能作为人工智能发展的关键领域，正面临数据异构性、强物理约束及交互昂贵等挑战，难以直接复制大语言模型的“大规模预训练+规模定律”范式。本文从数据、模型、系统与评测四个维度全面梳理了具身智能的前沿技术演进。在数据层面，文章提出了“数据金字塔”结构，主张利用底层庞大的仿真与互联网视频数据构建物理常识，通过中层人类交互数据进行行为映射，最终以顶层少量真机数据实现技能落地。在模型层面，探讨了主流视觉-语言-动作模型（VLA）的扩展瓶颈，并指出“世界模型”作为具身预训练的新方向，能够通过模拟环境动力学与未来预演，赋予智能体更强的物理直觉与泛化能力。在系统层面，观察到架构正从单一端到端模型向类操作系统的“分层架构”演进，实现高层语义规划与底层运动控制的解耦。最后，本文审视了当前评测体系在真实性与可复现性上的挑战，并对行走与操作一体化及具身智能“ImageNet时刻”的到来进行了展望。
